summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's surprise release of Safari at Macworld Expo 2003, explaining why Apple needed its own browser and why they chose the obscure KHTML engine over the expected Gecko/Mozilla. He argues that Mac OS X lacked a decent native browser — IE was ugly and tied Apple to Microsoft, Mozilla was a cross-platform monstrosity, OmniWeb had a broken rendering engine, and Opera required payment. Apple's choice of KHTML over Gecko came down to performance: KHTML was lean at 140,000 lines of code versus Gecko's 1.5 million, enabling dramatically faster startup and page load times. Even in public beta, Safari was already the fastest browser on the Mac.
key_points: Apple's dependence on IE as the default Mac browser meant dependence on Microsoft — an unacceptable business risk regardless of Microsoft's good faith
The two separate challenges of building a browser — the HTML rendering engine and the browser application — explain why no existing Mac option was satisfactory
Apple chose KHTML over Gecko because KHTML was 140,000 lines versus Gecko's 1.5 million, enabling superior performance and easier integration with Cocoa
Dave Hyatt's prescient 2002 critique of Gecko's architecture — its bloated cross-platform abstractions, poor threading model, and startup penalty — foreshadowed exactly why Apple avoided it
Safari's beta was already the fastest browser on Mac OS X by every measurable metric, addressing a real and publicly embarrassing performance gap
money_quotes: Remember the good old days, when betas were secret? Actually, what's changed is that what used to be called "1.0" is now called "public beta".
It's just good business sense to avoid any situation in which you are dependent upon Microsoft acting in good faith.
OmniWeb is a good browser application with a lousy HTML engine. Mozilla, on the other hand, is a terrible browser application (at least by Macintosh standards) with a very good HTML engine.
Good HTML rendering engines aren't exactly falling out of the sky.

A browser on OS X done right should be able to dust Gecko in terms of speed and footprint. It should be able to just smoke Gecko in startup time and page load time. The fact that this hasn't been done yet doesn't mean it can't be done.
Apple's stated goal is to make Safari the best browser on the Mac. What's unstated, but clearly their larger goal, is to make it the best browser in the world, period.
key_insight: Apple's choice of the obscure KHTML engine over the popular Gecko was a bold architectural bet that lean, clean code would outperform a bloated but feature-complete alternative — and even the public beta proved them right.
